title: Realtime Notes List Revalidation
motivation: The notes list should reflect server-side creates, updates, and deletes without manual refresh or client polling. This PRD introduces server-push change delivery over authenticated Server-Sent Events and hooks the UI into router data revalidation, while preserving per-user isolation, reconnect recovery, and existing auth semantics.

included: PostgreSQL-backed note change emission and per-worker fanout to subscribers.
Authenticated SSE delivery of note change events to active browser sessions.
Frontend stream subscription, router revalidation, reconnect recovery, auth refresh handling, and logout cleanup.
CI-gated cross-user isolation coverage for the new event transport.
excluded: Optimistic in-place patching of the notes cache.
Cross-device collaborative editing of a single open note.

design_facts.decision: PostgreSQL triggers emit note-change notifications for inserts, deletes, and title/content updates while excluding embedding-only updates.
design_facts.rationale: Background embedding writes should not create client-visible note-list churn.

design_facts.decision: Every uvicorn worker owns one LISTEN connection and an in-process per-user broker.
design_facts.rationale: Postgres NOTIFY provides cross-worker delivery while each worker handles its own connected clients.

design_facts.decision: Subscriber queues are bounded at fifty events and slow consumers are disconnected.
design_facts.rationale: A stalled browser tab must not block delivery to other subscribers.

design_facts.decision: The stream uses the standard Authorization bearer header and forbids query-string tokens.
design_facts.rationale: Bearer tokens in URLs would leak through proxy and access logs.

design_facts.decision: The SSE stream emits a thirty-second comment-line heartbeat.
design_facts.rationale: Idle streams need an observable keepalive and a cleanup cadence.

design_facts.decision: Client-visible events carry only kind and note_id while user_id stays server-side for routing.
design_facts.rationale: The client does not need routing identity, and omitting it narrows leakage risk.

design_facts.decision: The frontend revalidates on every stream open, including reconnect opens.
design_facts.rationale: Postgres NOTIFY is fire-and-forget, so reconnect open is the recovery boundary for missed events.

design_facts.decision: Reconnect attempts use exponential backoff starting at one second and capped at thirty seconds.
design_facts.rationale: Outages should not trigger tight reconnect loops.

design_facts.decision: A stream 401 triggers the existing token refresh path before reconnect; refresh failure logs out and stops reconnecting.
design_facts.rationale: The stream should reuse existing auth semantics instead of inventing a separate credential lifecycle.

design_facts.decision: Logout aborts the active event stream within one hundred milliseconds.
design_facts.rationale: Subscribers must not leak across logout/login cycles.

features.title: Postgres trigger emits NOTIFY on note title/content/delete

oracle.setup: Alembic migration applied against PostgreSQL with at least one note row available.
assertions: A committed note insert, title/content update, and delete each produce exactly one event on the intended Postgres notification channel.
A transaction rollback produces no delivered note-change event.
An update that does not touch note title/content/delete semantics does not produce client-visible notification noise.
oracle.tooling: pytest with an async PostgreSQL LISTEN connection.

features.title: Per-worker LISTEN consumer plus per-user broker fanout

contract.fanout_rule: route payloads only to subscribers matching payload.user_id

oracle.setup: FastAPI lifespan running with PostgreSQL NOTIFY available.
assertions: Each worker starts exactly one LISTEN connection on the note events channel.
A broker with subscribers for two users routes each payload only to subscribers for that payload's user.
A saturated subscriber queue is disconnected without preventing other subscribers from receiving events.
Two broker instances each receive every PostgreSQL notification, proving cross-worker fanout through Postgres.
oracle.tooling: pytest async integration tests with broker instances and PostgreSQL NOTIFY.

oracle.setup: Authenticated and unauthenticated HTTP clients plus an in-process broker test double.
assertions: GET `/notes/events` with a valid bearer token returns an SSE stream and registers one subscriber under the authenticated user id.
Missing or invalid bearer credentials receive 401 and do not register a subscriber.
Each emitted SSE event contains only the client-visible event fields, not the routing user id.
Client disconnect unregisters the subscriber from the broker.

features.title: SSE lifecycle heartbeat and disconnect cleanup

oracle.setup: Authenticated SSE stream connected and idle.
assertions: The stream emits a comment-line heartbeat every 30 seconds while idle.
After client TCP close, the subscriber is removed from the broker within one heartbeat interval.
FastAPI lifespan shutdown closes active streams and the LISTEN connection without pending task warnings.
oracle.tooling: pytest with async streaming client and fake or bounded timers.

features.title: Frontend SSE client with bearer auth

oracle.setup: Mocked fetch-stream transport and auth store token state.
assertions: Opening a stream calls the transport with the same bearer Authorization header used by REST requests.
Injected SSE frames invoke the subscriber callback once per frame with parsed event payload.
Calling dispose aborts the underlying fetch and stops forwarding events.

features.title: Notes loader revalidation on received events

oracle.setup: Notes route rendered with a mocked router and the notes event stream subscriber attached.
assertions: Injecting three received events calls `router.revalidate()` exactly three times.
Creating a note through a separate authenticated client causes the rendered notes list to show the new note within two seconds.
During a 30 second idle window before mutation, the browser makes no polling-style requests to `/api/notes`.
oracle.tooling: Vitest route test plus browser smoke.

oracle.setup: Notes event stream client connected to router revalidation with controllable open/close events.
assertions: The client invokes `router.revalidate()` immediately on initial stream open.
After stream close and re-open, the client invokes `router.revalidate()` again.
After backend interruption and missed mutation, the rendered notes list reflects the mutation within two seconds of stream re-open without manual action.
oracle.tooling: Vitest fake stream lifecycle plus browser smoke.

oracle.setup: Notes event stream client under fake timers with controllable transport failures.
assertions: After a stream close, reconnect attempts occur at one, three, seven, fifteen, thirty-one, and sixty-one seconds.
Reconnect delay caps at thirty seconds after repeated failures.
Backoff resets to one second after a successful stream open that remains stable for at least thirty seconds.

features.title: SSE auth-failure refresh and logout handling

oracle.setup: Mocked auth store, mocked stream response, and fake timers.
assertions: A 401 stream response invokes the existing token refresh path before the next reconnect attempt.
After refresh succeeds, the client reconnects with the new bearer token and resets backoff.
After refresh fails, logout is invoked exactly once and no further reconnect attempts are scheduled.
oracle.tooling: Vitest with mocked auth store and fake timers.

oracle.setup: Active notes event stream subscription and mocked auth store state.
assertions: When authentication transitions to false, the active stream is aborted within 100ms.
After teardown, no further events are forwarded to subscribers.
A backend smoke check observes broker subscriber count decrement after logout.
oracle.tooling: Vitest fake timers plus backend smoke.

oracle.setup: Two authenticated users, an active SSE stream for user B, and real HTTP note mutation calls for user A and user B.
assertions: User B's stream registers with the broker before the isolation assertions begin.
Ten interleaved mutations by user A produce zero events on user B's stream during the five second observation window.
One mutation by user B produces exactly one event on user B's stream with the expected payload.
The test is wired into CI as a required gate.
oracle.tooling: pytest async integration test with httpx streaming client.
